label: PAC Learning
definition: Leslie Valiant's framework defining what it means to learn from examples: with high probability, output a hypothesis whose error is small, using a sample that grows only polynomially in the relevant parameters. Both hedges, "probably" and "approximately," are unavoidable for a learner that sees only a finite random sample, and a class is learnable exactly when its VC dimension is finite.

body.text: PAC learning, short for Probably Approximately Correct, gives a precise mathematical answer to a question that sounds hopelessly informal: when can we say an algorithm has actually learned something from data? Leslie Valiant's 1984 framework asks the learner to be approximately correct, its error below a tolerance epsilon, and to be so only probably, allowing it to fail with small probability delta. Both slacks are necessary, not concessions: a learner sees a finite random sample, so it can always be unlucky in its draw or land just short of perfect, and a definition of learning that forbade either would be satisfiable by nothing.

The setup is clean. An unknown target concept is drawn from a known concept class, and labeled examples arrive from some fixed but unknown distribution. A concept class is PAC learnable if some algorithm, for any target, any distribution, and any chosen epsilon and delta, returns a hypothesis with error at most epsilon with probability at least one minus delta. The crucial demand is efficiency: the number of examples and the running time must scale polynomially in one over epsilon, one over delta, and the problem size, because learnability that needs exponentially many examples is not learning in any useful sense.

The framework matters because it separates what is learnable in principle from what is not, and it does so distribution-free, assuming nothing about the data beyond that train and test come from the same source. This is what ties it to generalization: a PAC guarantee is a worst-case bound on the gap between performance on the sample and on the full distribution, and sample complexity gets its formal meaning here as the count of examples sufficient to hit the epsilon and delta targets. The theory's deepest result then ties learnability to a single number, the VC dimension: a class is PAC learnable with finite sample complexity if and only if its VC dimension is finite, with the sample count growing roughly linearly in that dimension. This is why VC dimension is the central measure of capacity, and it is why minimizing error on the training sample, empirical risk minimization, suffices for a guarantee whenever capacity is bounded.

PAC learning has limits later theory addressed, and naming them is part of using it honestly. The original model assumes a realizable case where some concept fits perfectly, which the agnostic extension relaxes to let the best hypothesis still err; and being a worst-case framework over all distributions, it can be pessimistic about the friendly distributions of real life, a pessimism the no free lunch theorem shows is partly unavoidable without further assumptions. Even so, PAC learning remains the bedrock on which the modern vocabulary of learnability, capacity, and generalization is built.
